title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

texts.A: The deep and enduring human drive to explore and potentially colonize Mars stands as a testament to the remarkable and admirable spirit of our species, but we must exercise critical judgment and ensure that our romantic 'backup planet' ambitions do not serve as a convenient psychological distraction from our urgent and far more immediate civilizational responsibilities on Earth. [38]Transforming a frozen, radiation-bathed, atmospherically thin desert into a genuinely habitable world for human communities would require centuries of speculative terraforming—a process that remains entirely in the realm of theoretical possibility and technological conjecture given our current scientific capabilities.[/38] [37]To dedicate hundreds of billions of dollars to an uncertain Martian colonial project while our own planetary oceans are measurably rising, our forests are burning at record rates, and our ecosystems are collapsing is not merely an eccentricity of the wealthy; it represents a profound dereliction of our fundamental duty to the only demonstrably habitable home our species has ever known.[/37]
texts.B: [39]Establishing a permanently inhabited multi-planetary human presence is arguably the only genuinely reliable long-term strategy for ensuring the continuation and ultimate survival of our species against the full spectrum of potential existential risks, whether naturally occurring such as asteroid impacts and supervolcanic eruptions, or entirely man-made through warfare, ecological collapse, or catastrophic pandemic.[/39] The advanced technologies necessarily developed and rigorously refined in the process of pursuing a Mars settlement programme—ultra-efficient water recycling systems, genuinely closed-loop agricultural systems, and compact high-efficiency renewable energy solutions—will have immediate, profound, and direct practical benefits for sustainability and resource management here on Earth, long before the first human colonist sets foot on Martian soil. [37]Far from representing a self-indulgent distraction from terrestrial environmental priorities and urgent climate action, the Mars programme is a powerful and inspiring catalyst for precisely the transformative technological innovations we most urgently need to preserve our own fragile biosphere.[/37] We are not choosing between Earth and Mars but between stagnation and genuine progress.
texts.C: I agree that the fundamental 'survival of the species' argument for Mars colonization carries genuine philosophical weight and deserves to be taken seriously rather than dismissed. However, we must honestly address the deeply uncomfortable question of potential 'biological imperialism' embedded in the very premise of our presence on another planet. [40]If there exists even a microscopic trace of indigenous microbial life anywhere on the Red Planet—a genuine scientific possibility that cannot be dismissed—our uncontrolled physical arrival there would almost certainly destroy or irreversibly contaminate it before we even had the opportunity to recognize its extraordinary significance.[/40] I share the view that we should maintain an exclusive priority on sophisticated robotic exploration in the near term. However, [39]I disagree that the financial costs associated with space exploration are inherently unjustifiable; when compared honestly and proportionately to global annual military expenditure, the entire budget for scientific space exploration represents a genuinely small investment.[/39] The pressing question is ultimately governance and ownership of any extraterrestrial settlement.
texts.D: [40]The profound psychological toll and severe physiological consequences of extended human habitation on Mars are systematically and conveniently overlooked in the glossy promotional brochures and enthusiastic investor presentations of private space companies seeking funding.[/40] Human beings are the product of millions of years of careful evolutionary adaptation to Earth's specific gravitational field, atmospheric composition, magnetic shielding, and rich social environment; attempting to sustain normal physiological and psychological health in a confined pressurized habitat for years would inevitably lead to profound musculoskeletal decay, neurological changes, and severe psychological deterioration that current mitigation strategies cannot adequately address. [38]While I share the broader skepticism regarding the feasibility and timeline of Mars terraforming as currently presented, I believe the strategically and scientifically sound immediate priority should be the establishment and sustained operation of a genuinely international lunar base.[/38] Mars represents a bridge demonstrably too far for a species that has not yet learned to live sustainably and cooperatively within the carrying capacity of its own home planet.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the relationship between Mars exploration and Earth's sustainability?
questions.explanation: Expert B argues that Mars technology will help Earth's sustainability. Expert A argues that focusing on Mars is a 'distraction' and a 'dereliction of duty' to our current climate crisis.

questions.question: Which expert shares A's opinion about the feasibility or timeline of terraforming Mars?
questions.explanation: Expert A calls terraforming 'technologically speculative at best.' Expert D also 'shares the skepticism regarding the terraforming timeline.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to B regarding the necessity of space investment for human progress?
questions.explanation: Expert B sees it as choosing a 'future of infinite potential.' Expert C agrees the budget is a 'pittance' compared to other spending and finds the survival argument 'compelling.'

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on the primary risk or 'real issue' of Mars missions?
questions.explanation: Expert C focuses on 'biological imperialism' and ownership models. Expert D focuses on the 'psychological and physical toll' and the 'physiological decay' of humans themselves.
